title: L05.3.io and paths
body:
Toolkit for Finance and Financial Decision Making
Lecture 5.3: IO and paths

Breno Schmidt and Robert Tumarkin
UNSW




                                                    1
 Input/Output (IO)
 Input/Output (IO)




 Most code we write for Financial analysis needs to work with information stored in files:

      Financial Data: Datasets with stock prices, trading volumes, or economic indicators are
      commonly stored in CSV or text files.
      Configuration Files: Placing important parameters and confidential information in code is
      inadvisable. Configuration files can store things API keys, passwords, or data paths to
      made code more flexible and secure.
      Saving Results: Analysis created by our programs need to be saved so they can be shared
      or analysed further.

     While Python permits us to work with a large number of file types, this class will focus on reading
                                information from and writing to text files.




                                                                                                           2
 Input/Output (IO): Reading files

 Reading from a file traditionally involves three steps:

          1      file = open(file_path, "r")
          2      contents = file.read()
          3      file.close()


 Steps:
   1. open(file_path, mode) -> <io.TextIOWrapper>:
         Opens the file at the specified file_path location. This is usually a string (str) or a filepath
              (Path) object.
           We indicate to Python that we want to read a file with the mode parameter "r".
           Returns an file object, raising an error if there is a problem. In the case of a text file, Python
              returns a file object of the io.TextIOWrapper class.
           In this case, we open a file and assign the io.TextIOWrapper instance to the name file.
   2. <io.TextIOWrapper>.read() -> str
         Reads all the information from the file object.
         In this case of <io.TextIOWrapper>, this returns a str that assigned to the name contents.
   3. <io.TextIOWrapper>.close() -> None
         Closes the file.
         This is an important step. Closing the file also helps prevent data corruption, ensures efficient
              resource, management and permits other programs to access the data while the Python program
              continues.

                                          Always close files after use!


                                                                                                                 3
 Input/Output (IO): Reading files




 There are three methods that read text from a file object:

 <io.TextIoWrapper>.read() -> str
 Read the entire file into a string.

 <io.TextIoWrapper>.readlines() -> [str]
 Read the entire file into a list of strings, one for each line.

 <io.TextIoWrapper>.readline() -> str
 Read a single line from the file. Typically used in while loops.




                                                                    4
 Input/Output (IO): Reading files




 However, file iteration allows Python to process one line at a time:

         1     filename = 'yourfile.txt'
         2     file = open(filename, "r")
         3     for line in file:
         4         <code_block_to_process_line>
         5
         6     file.close()



                        File iteration is generally the best approach for reading a file.




                                                                                            5
 Input/Output (IO): Reading files




 Once a file has been read, Python cannot read it again:


         1     filename = 'yourfile.txt'
         2     file = open(filename, "r")
         3     first_read_contents = file.read()
         4     second_read_contents = file.read()
         5     file.close()



                          second_read_contents will be the empty string, ""


 .




                                                                              6
 Input/Output (IO): Reading files




 Close and reopen a file that you need to read twice:


         1     filename = 'yourfile.txt'
         2     file = open(filename, "r")
         3     first_read_contents = file.read()
         4     file.close()
         5
         6     file = open(filename, "r")
         7     second_read_contents = file.read()
         8     file.close()




                                                        7
 Input/Output (IO): Writing files

 Writing to a file traditionally involves three steps:

          1      file = open(file_path, "w")
          2      file.write("Hello, world!\n")
          3      file.write("Goodbye from Python.\n")
          4      file.close()


 Steps:
   1. open(file_path, mode) -> <io.TextIOWrapper>:
         Opens the file at the specified file_path location. This is usually a string (str) or a filepath
              (Path) object.
           We indicate to Python that we want to write to a file with the mode parameter:
                • "w" will overwrite any existing existing file.
                • "x" will throw an error if the file already exists.
           Returns an file object, raising an error if there is a problem. In the case of a text file, Python
              returns a file object of the io.TextIOWrapper class.
   2. <io.TextIOWrapper>.write(str) -> int
         Writes <str> to the file object.
         Including a newline character "\n" in a string ensures that any subsequent write statement
              occurs on a new line. Try the code above without the newline characters.
           Returns an int indicating the number of characters written to the file. This can be ignored in
              many cases, such as those above.
   3. <io.TextIOWrapper>.close() -> None
         Closes the file.
         This is an important step that ensures all the data is written to the file should the program
              eventually crash. Closing the file also helps prevent data corruption, ensures efficient resource,
              management and permits other programs to access the data while the Python program continues.
                                                                                                                   8
 Input/Output (IO): Writing files




    There are two methods that write text from a file object:

    <io.TextIoWrapper>.write(str) -> int
    Write a single string to a file. Add newline characters “\n” to ensure multiple strings are placed on separate
    lines. Returns the number of characters written.

    <io.TextIoWrapper>.writelines(iterable_str) -> None
    Write a multiple lines to a file. Expects an iterable of strings, such as [str]. Add newline characters “\n” to
    each string to place each on separate lines. Include a newline on the last string in the iterable if you expect
    to write additional information to the file.
1       lines = ["apple\n", "banana\n", "cherry\n"]
2       file.writelines(lines)




                                                                                                                      9
 Input/Output (IO): Context managers




 Context managers are the recommended way to handle IO in Python:

        1     with open(file_path, mode) as file:
        2         <indented_code_block>

 The context manager ensures that the file is closed automatically for you.
     During normal execution, the context manager will close the file when evaluation reaches
     the end of the <indented_code_block>.
     If there is an exception, Python will close the file as it terminates.

                  Context managers should be used for both reading and writing tasks.




                                                                                                10
 Input/Output (IO): Best practice




 When reading files, the best practice for most applications is a context manager along with file iteration:


         1     with open(file_path, "r") as file:
         2         for line in file:
         3             <code_block>



 When writing files, the best practice for most applications is a context manager along with .write() and
 .writelines() methods:

         1     # Either "x" or "w" as the write_mode as appropriate
         2     with open(file_path, write_mode) as file:
         3           <code_block>




                                                                                                               11
 Filepaths
 Filepaths




 A filepath specifies the unique location of a file within a computer’s file system hierarchy:

      A Filepaths shows the route through directories or folders to reach a particular file.
      Directory names (folders) are listed in order and separated by delimiters that depend on
      the operating system.
      There are two main types of filepaths:
          Absolute filepath: Provides the complete location from the root directory to the file, independent
            of the current working directory.
          Relative filepath: Specifies the location relative to the current working directory, not starting
            from the root.




                                                                                                                12
 Filepaths




 Filepaths are system specific:

 Unix and MacOS:
      Absolute paths begin with a forward slash (/)
      Relative paths begins with a directory name
      Directories are separated by forward slashes (/)

 Windows:
      Absolute paths begin with a device specifier (e.g., c:\)
      Relative paths begin with a directory name
      Directories are separated by backslashes (\)




                                                                 13
 Filepaths




 Consider an example in which projects is a top-level directory:

      CSV file:
         projects -> prop_trading -> data -> stocks.csv
      Current working directory:
         projects -> prop_trading

 The absolute and relative paths:

      Unix and MacOS
         Absolute path: /projects/prop_trading/data/stocks.csv
         Relative path: data/stocks.csv
      Windows
         Absolute path: c:\projects\prop_trading\data\stocks.csv
         Relative path: data\stocks.csv




                                                                    14
 Filepaths




 We’d like our code to be portable so it functions on different computers and platforms, but using strings for
 paths is problematic:

      Strings are platform-specific, requiring a program adjusts for forward and backslashes
      automatically.
      Paths may be corrupted by tab “\t” and newline “\n” characters.
          Python can misinterpret a path like “c:\the_directory\new_file.csv” as having a tab character
            and a newline.
          Double backslashes solves this problem, but is error-prone if the programmer forgets.


        The solution is to use Python’s pathlib module to create cross-platform, error-free filepaths




                                                                                                                 15
 Filepaths: The pathlib module and Path class




 The pathlib module provides a Path class that solves the platform-specific path problem:

         1     from pathlib import Path
         2     p = Path("directory_or_file_name")


    When a Path is used in an open statement, Python will adjust the path for platform-specific details.




                                                                                                           16
 Filepaths: The pathlib module and Path class




 The pathlib module provides two ways to construct paths:

 Path / str -> Path
 Combines a Path with a string (or Path) into a new Path.

      1     p = Path("base_dir") / "sub_dir" / "file.ext"
      2     p = Path("base_dir") / Path("sub_dir") / Path("file.ext")


 joinpath(*other) -> Path
 Joins one or more path components.

      1     p = Path("base_dir").joinpath("sub_dir", "file.ext")



                 Each Path object or string should contain a single directory level or file.




                                                                                               17
 Filepaths: Common Path methods



    The Path class provides several utility functions to inspect the kind of information that exists at a given
    filepath.

    path_obj.exists() –> bool
    Checks if the path exists in the filesystem.
1       Path("base_dir").joinpath("sub_dir", "file.ext").exists()
2       # True if the file exists at the path, and False otherwise.


    path_obj.is_dir() –> bool
    Checks if the path points to a directory.
1       Path("base_dir").joinpath("sub_dir", "other_dir").is_dir()
2       # True if the path exists and is a directory, and False otherwise.


    path_obj.is_file() –> bool
    Checks if the path points to a file.
1       Path("base_dir").joinpath("sub_dir", "file.ext").is_dir()
2       # True if the path exists and is a file, and False otherwise.




                                                                                                                  18
 Filepaths: Common Path attributes



    The Path class provides attributes to extract individual components of a filepath.

    path_obj.name –> str
    Returns the final component (file or directory name) of the path.
1       p = Path("base_dir").joinpath("sub_dir", "file.ext")
2       p.name # "file.ext"


    path_obj.stem
    Returns the file name without its extension.
1       p.stem # "file"


    path_obj.suffix
    Returns the file extension with the leading dot.
1       p.suffix # ".ext"


    path_obj.parent -> Path
    Returns the directory containing the file or directory.
1       p.parent # PathType('base_dir/sub_dir')



                                                                                         19
 Input/Output (IO) and Filepaths: Best practice
 Input/Output (IO) and Filepaths: Best practice



 When reading files, the best practice for most applications is using a Path object in a context manager
 along with file iteration:


    1     from pathlib import Path
    2
    3     file_path = Path("base_dir").joinpath("sub_dir", "file.ext")
    4     with open(file_path, "r") as file:
    5         for line in file:
    6             <code_block>



 When writing files, the best practice for most applications is using a Path object in context manager along
 with .write() and .writelines() methods:

    1     from pathlib import Path
    2
    3     file_path = Path("base_dir").joinpath("sub_dir", "file.ext")
    4
    5     # Either "x" or "w" as the write_mode as appropriate
    6     with open(file_path, write_mode) as file:
    7           <code_block>




                                                                                                               20
